tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.
 - conj_grad(int*, int*, double*, double*, double*, double*, double*, double*, double*): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_1>>
 - timer_read(int): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_2>>
 - timer_start(int): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_3>>
 - timer_stop(int): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_4>>
 - unknown: 21 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_5>>
hint.title: CALL instructions

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
 - IDIV: 5 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_1>>
 - JMP: 15 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_2>>
 - UCOMISD: 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_3>>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround:
- Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * Please look into your compiler manual for march=native or equivalent
 - Use vector aligned instructions:
  1) align your arrays on 32 bytes boundaries
  2) inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: read your compiler manual.
<<image_vec_align>>
hint.title: Vector unaligned load/store instructions
hint.txt: Detected 5 suboptimal vector unaligned load/store instructions.

hint.workaround: Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

hint.txt:
4 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).
3 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (two at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your function (in the source code) and the binary function
hint.txt:
The binary function is composed of 9 FP arithmetical operations:
 - 4: addition or subtraction
 - 3: multiply
 - 1: divide
 - 1: square root
The binary function is loading 557 bytes (69 double precision FP elements).
The binary function is storing 249 bytes (31 double precision FP elements).

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 139.50 cycles. At this rate:
 - 6% of peak load performance is reached (3.99 out of 64.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 5% of peak store performance is reached (1.78 out of 32.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.help: List of bottlenecks detected by CQA. A bottleneck is an issue which reduces front-end throughput below the core capacity.
expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks

header:
Warnings:
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [ENDBR64] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [CLTD] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [CLTQ] is unknown
 - Detected a function call instruction: ignoring called function instructions.
Rerun with --follow-calls=append to include them to analysis  or with --follow-calls=inline to simulate inlining.
0% of peak computational performance is used (0.06 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
- Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 139.50 to 21.33 cycles (6.54x speedup).

gain.workaround:
- Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
 - Make array accesses unit-stride:
  * If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA)
gain.details:
14% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
 - 9% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
 - 18% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
 - 50% of SSE/AVX multiply instructions are used in vector version.
 - 0% of SSE/AVX divide and square root instructions are used in vector version.
 - 8% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized function can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your function is not vectorized.
Only 24% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 139.50 to 19.25 cycles (7.25x speedup).

gain.workaround:
- Reduce the number of division or square root instructions:
  * If denominator is constant over iterations, use reciprocal (replace x/y with x*(1/y)). Check precision impact.
 - Check whether you really need double precision. If not, switch to single precision to speedup execution
gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt:
Performance is limited by execution of divide and square root operations (the divide/square root unit is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 139.50 to 102.50 cycles (1.36x speedup).

potential.title: Expensive FP math instructions/calls
potential.txt:
Detected performance impact from expensive FP math instructions/calls.
By removing/reexpressing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 139.50 to 101.67 cycles (1.37x speedup).

potential.workaround:
- Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * Please look into your compiler manual for march=native or equivalent
 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).
potential.title: FMA
potential.txt: Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
